title: Prisms & the Cube
blurb: The box family: what a prism is, the cube's two famous diagonals (√2 and √3), and surface area & volume you can trust because you can see every face.
buildsOn: Unit 1's perpendicular-and-oblique triangle; areas of rectangles and triangles; the Pythagorean theorem.

lessons.title: What Is a Prism
lessons.concreteComparison: A prism is a cookie-cutter shape pushed through dough: whatever polygon you start with slides along a straight line, and every slice along the way is that same polygon. A tent, a Toblerone bar, a skyscraper — same recipe, different cookie cutter.
lessons.objective: Name the parts of a prism (bases, lateral faces, edges, height), distinguish right from oblique prisms, and count elements.
concept: Take a polygon (the **base**), make an identical copy in a parallel plane, and join matching corners. That's a **prism**. The two copies are its bases; the connecting parallelograms are **lateral faces**; the connecting segments are **lateral edges**. All lateral edges are parallel and equal — they're the same slide repeated.

If the lateral edges are **perpendicular to the bases**, the prism is a **right prism** (lateral faces are rectangles; the Toblerone standing straight). Tilted, and it's an **oblique prism** (a leaning stack of cards). The **height** is always the DISTANCE between base planes — for a right prism that's the lateral edge itself; for an oblique one it's shorter than the edge.
Counting rules for a prism on an $n$-gon: $2n$ vertices, $n$ lateral faces ($n + 2$ faces total), and $3n$ edges ($n$ per base, $n$ lateral). A triangular prism: $6$ vertices, $5$ faces, $9$ edges.
lessons.keyIdea: Prism = polygon slid to a parallel copy. Right prism: slide is perpendicular (faces are rectangles). Height = distance between base planes, not necessarily the lateral edge.

facts.title: Element counts
facts.latex: n\text{-gon base}: \; 2n \text{ vertices}, \; n+2 \text{ faces}, \; 3n \text{ edges}
facts.explanation: n per base ring, n lateral — everything comes in rings of n.

workedExamples.statement: A prism has a hexagonal base. Count its vertices, edges, and faces, and verify Euler's formula $V - E + F = 2$.
An oblique prism has lateral edge $10$ making a $60°$ angle with the base plane. Find the prism's height.
workedExamples.solution: The height is the perpendicular between the base planes — Unit 1's triangle: $h = 10\sin 60° = 10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt3}{2} = 5\sqrt{3} \approx 8.66$. The lean costs height: $5\sqrt3 < 10$.

commonMistakes.text: Using the lateral edge as the height of an oblique prism.
commonMistakes.correction: Height = perpendicular DISTANCE between base planes. Only in a right prism does the lateral edge run perpendicular; in an oblique prism, height = edge × sin(angle with base) < edge.

commonMistakes.text: Counting a prism's faces as just the lateral ones and forgetting the two bases.
commonMistakes.correction: Faces = n lateral + 2 bases = n + 2. The bases are faces too — a closed box has a top and a bottom.

tryIt.statement: A prism has $24$ edges. What polygon is its base, and how many vertices and faces does it have?
tryIt.solution: $3n = 24$ ⟹ $n = 8$: an octagonal base. Vertices: $2n = 16$; faces: $n + 2 = 10$. Euler: $16 - 24 + 10 = 2$ ✓.

tryIt.statement: A right prism's lateral edge is $7$. What is its height? And if the same-edge prism were tilted to $30°$ above the base plane, what would the height become?
tryIt.solution: Right prism: height = lateral edge = $7$. Tilted to $30°$: $h = 7\sin 30° = 3.5$ — half the edge. Tilting a prism flattens it.

steps.title: Slide a polygon, get a prism
steps.body: Base triangle $ABC$ below, identical copy $A'B'C'$ above, corners joined. The joins $AA'$, $BB'$, $CC'$ are the **lateral edges** — all parallel, all equal (each is 'the slide'). Each lateral face, like $ABB'A'$, is a parallelogram. In the drawing, hidden edges of the far side are dashed.

steps.title: The triangular prism, live
steps.teach: Step the base and length and watch the volume respond. Every slice perpendicular to the slide is the same triangle — that's why volume will turn out to be (slice area) × (slide length) in lesson 4.

steps.title: Standing straight or leaning
steps.body: Both prisms below have the same base segment and the same-length lateral edges — but the right prism's edges rise perpendicular to the base (height = edge), while the oblique prism leans (height < edge). Height is ALWAYS measured as the perpendicular distance between base planes, shown dashed on the oblique one.

steps.title: The leaning prism
steps.prompt: An oblique prism's lateral edge is $10$, making a $30°$ angle with the base plane. Its height is…
steps.explanation: Height $= 10 \sin 30° = 5$. The edge is the oblique, the height is the perpendicular — Unit 1's triangle exactly. ($5\sqrt3 \approx 8.66$ would be the $60°$ lean.)

steps.title: Counting and leaning
steps.intro: Ring-of-n counting, then the height triangle.

examples.prompt: Hexagonal prism: vertices, edges, faces? Euler check.
Lateral edge $10$ at $60°$ to the base. Height?

steps.title: Prism anatomy
steps.intro: Everything comes in rings of n.

problems.prompt: A prism has $15$ edges. Its base is a…
A right prism's height is $8$ and its lateral edge is…
problems.explanation: In a RIGHT prism the lateral edge IS perpendicular to the bases, so edge = height = 8.

problems.prompt: An oblique prism with lateral edge $6$ has height $3\sqrt2$. Its lean angle above the base is…

steps.title: Prism basics
bullets: Prism = base polygon + parallel copy + parallelogram walls; lateral edges all parallel & equal.
n-gon base ⟹ 2n vertices, 3n edges, n+2 faces (Euler: V − E + F = 2).
Right prism: edges ⊥ base, faces are rectangles, height = edge.
Oblique prism: height = edge × sin(lean) — always less than the edge.

lessons.title: The Cube & Its Diagonals
lessons.concreteComparison: What's the longest pencil that fits in a cubic box? Not the edge, not the diagonal drawn on the bottom — it's the corner-to-opposite-corner line through the AIR inside. For a box of side 10, the floor diagonal is 14.1 but the air diagonal is 17.3. Two different diagonals, two different jobs.
lessons.objective: Compute a cube's face diagonal (a√2) and space diagonal (a√3) by running Pythagoras twice, and extend to the general box diagonal.
concept: A cube of edge $a$ has two kinds of diagonal. The **face diagonal** lies on a face: it's the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $a, a$, so $d_{face} = a\sqrt{2}$ — the 45-45-90 pattern from plane geometry.

The **space diagonal** cuts through the interior from a corner to the opposite corner. Find it with Pythagoras TWICE: first across the floor ($a\sqrt2$), then stand up the height: $d^2 = (a\sqrt2)^2 + a^2 = 3a^2$, so $d_{space} = a\sqrt{3}$. The right angle in the second step is guaranteed by Unit 1: the vertical edge is perpendicular to the whole floor, hence to the floor diagonal.

The same double-Pythagoras solves ANY box (rectangular parallelepiped) with edges $l, w, h$: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2}$. This is the single most-used formula of the unit — and it's just Pythagoras run twice.
lessons.keyIdea: Face diagonal a√2, space diagonal a√3; for any box d = √(l² + w² + h²) — Pythagoras across the floor, then Pythagoras up the wall.

facts.title: Cube diagonals
facts.explanation: √2 on a face, √3 through the air.

facts.title: Why it works
facts.explanation: The vertical edge ⊥ floor ⟹ right angle with the floor diagonal.

workedExamples.statement: A cube has edge $6$. Find its face diagonal and space diagonal, exactly.
workedExamples.solution: Face: $6\sqrt{2} \approx 8.49$. Space: run Pythagoras twice — $d^2 = (6\sqrt2)^2 + 6^2 = 72 + 36 = 108$, $d = \sqrt{108} = 6\sqrt{3} \approx 10.39$. As expected, edge < face diagonal < space diagonal.

workedExamples.statement: A box measures $3 \times 4 \times 12$. Find its space diagonal.
workedExamples.solution: $d = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13$. (A whole-number space diagonal! $3, 4, 12, 13$ is a Pythagorean QUADRUPLE — the floor diagonal is the familiar $5$, and then $5, 12, 13$ finishes the climb.)

commonMistakes.text: Using a√2 for the space diagonal (or a√3 for the face diagonal).
commonMistakes.correction: Count the dimensions the diagonal crosses: a FACE diagonal crosses 2 edges' worth of directions ($\sqrt{a^2+a^2} = a\sqrt2$); the SPACE diagonal crosses all 3 ($\sqrt{3a^2} = a\sqrt3$). More directions, bigger root.

commonMistakes.text: Adding the three edges' squares only for cubes, but improvising for boxes.
commonMistakes.correction: $d = \sqrt{l^2 + w^2 + h^2}$ works for EVERY box, cube included ($l=w=h=a$ gives $a\sqrt3$). One formula, no special cases.

tryIt.statement: A cube's space diagonal is $9$. Find its edge.
Will a $17$-unit rod fit inside a $9 \times 12 \times 8$ box?
tryIt.solution: Longest fit = space diagonal $= \sqrt{81 + 144 + 64} = \sqrt{289} = 17$. It fits EXACTLY — corner to opposite corner, touching both.

steps.title: On the face vs through the air
steps.body: Cube $ABCDA'B'C'D'$, edge $a$. The **face diagonal** $AC$ (blue) lies flat on the bottom face. The **space diagonal** $AC'$ (accent, drawn dashed since it passes through the interior) flies from the front-bottom-left corner to the back-top-right. Different creatures: $AC = a\sqrt2$, $AC' = a\sqrt3$.

steps.title: The climb: floor first, then up
steps.body: To reach $C'$ from $A$: cross the floor along $AC = a\sqrt2$, then climb the vertical edge $CC' = a$. Unit 1 guarantees the right angle at $C$ (vertical edge ⊥ floor ⟹ ⊥ floor diagonal). The true-shape climb triangle for $a = 6$: legs $6\sqrt2 \approx 8.49$ and $6$, hypotenuse $\sqrt{72 + 36} = \sqrt{108} = 6\sqrt3$. That's the whole derivation — no formula memorization needed, just two Pythagoras steps.

steps.title: √2 or √3?
steps.prompt: Edge $5$ cube. The longest straight segment that fits INSIDE the cube has length…
steps.explanation: Longest fit = space diagonal $= 5\sqrt3 \approx 8.66$. $5\sqrt2$ only crosses a face; $15$ would be the three edges laid end to end, but a straight rod can't turn corners.

steps.title: Any box: d = √(l² + w² + h²)
steps.body: The double climb works for every rectangular box: floor diagonal $\sqrt{l^2 + w^2}$, then $d^2 = l^2 + w^2 + h^2$. Beautiful special case: the $3 \times 4 \times 12$ box. Floor: $\sqrt{9+16} = 5$. Climb: $\sqrt{25 + 144} = 13$. Whole numbers all the way — a Pythagorean quadruple $(3,4,12,13)$. Others to spot in problems: $(1,2,2,3)$, $(2,3,6,7)$, $(4,4,7,9)$, $(2,6,9,11)$.

steps.title: Rod-in-box problems
steps.intro: Space diagonal = the longest straight fit.

problems.prompt: A cube's FACE diagonal is $10$. The edge is…

steps.title: The √3 in your TV box
steps.body: Shipping companies quietly use the space diagonal: a '55-inch' TV (diagonal-measured screen) ships in a box whose space diagonal must beat 55 inches by enough margin for padding. And the ratio $\sqrt3 : \sqrt2 : 1 \approx 1.73 : 1.41 : 1$ means the air diagonal beats the floor diagonal by about 22% in any cube — free extra length, if you tilt your cargo.

steps.title: Diagonal toolkit
bullets: Face diagonal: a√2 (two directions). Space diagonal: a√3 (all three).
Any box: d = √(l² + w² + h²) — Pythagoras across the floor, then up.
The right angle for the climb comes from Unit 1: vertical edge ⊥ floor plane.

lessons.title: Surface Area of Prisms
lessons.concreteComparison: Wrapping a gift isn't about how much fits inside the box — it's about paper. Unfold the box flat and the mystery vanishes: the wrapping is two copies of the base plus one long rectangle (the walls, unrolled). Surface area is always just 'flatten it and add.'
lessons.objective: Compute lateral and total surface area of right prisms via the unfolded net: S_lat = (base perimeter) × height.
concept: Unfold (develop) a right prism: the lateral faces flatten into ONE rectangle whose width is the **base perimeter** $P$ and whose height is the prism's height $h$. So the lateral surface is $S_{lat} = P \cdot h$ — one multiplication, no matter how many faces.
Total surface adds the two bases: $S = P h + 2B$, where $B$ is the base area. For a box $l \times w \times h$: $S = 2(lw + lh + wh)$ — each pair of opposite faces counted once, doubled.
The net is the honest picture: every square unit of material appears exactly once, nothing hidden. When a problem asks 'how much paint/cardboard/steel,' it's asking for the net's area.
lessons.keyIdea: Unroll the walls into one rectangle: S_lat = P·h. Add the two bases for the total: S = P·h + 2B.

facts.title: Lateral surface
facts.explanation: Perimeter times height — the unrolled wall rectangle.

workedExamples.statement: Find the total surface area of a $3 \times 4 \times 5$ box.
workedExamples.solution: $S = 2(lw + lh + wh) = 2(12 + 15 + 20) = 2 \cdot 47 = 94$. Sanity check by nets: two $3\times4$, two $3\times5$, two $4\times5$: $24 + 30 + 40 = 94$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: A right prism of height $10$ stands on a right-triangle base with legs $6$ and $8$. Find its lateral and total surface area.
workedExamples.solution: Base hypotenuse: $\sqrt{36+64} = 10$, so perimeter $P = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24$ and $S_{lat} = P h = 240$. Base area $B = \frac{6 \cdot 8}{2} = 24$, total $S = 240 + 2 \cdot 24 = 288$.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the bases (or counting only one).
commonMistakes.correction: A closed prism has TWO bases. Lateral = walls only; total = walls + 2B. Read the problem: an open-top aquarium wants walls + 1 base; a sealed box wants walls + 2.

commonMistakes.text: Using a slant edge as the height in S_lat = P·h for an oblique prism.
commonMistakes.correction: $P \cdot h$ is a RIGHT-prism formula (walls unroll into a clean rectangle only then). Oblique prisms need face-by-face work — on exams, that's your signal the prism was meant to be right.

tryIt.statement: A cube has total surface area $150$. Find its edge and its face diagonal.
A hexagonal pencil (right prism) has base perimeter $12$ mm and length $180$ mm. How much paint covers its lateral surface?
tryIt.solution: $S_{lat} = P h = 12 \cdot 180 = 2160$ mm². (The two hexagonal ends are usually cut, not painted — lateral only is exactly right here.)

steps.title: The net tells the truth
steps.body: Surface area hides in 3D and confesses in 2D. Unfold a right prism: the walls become ONE rectangle of width = base perimeter, height = prism height; the two bases hinge off it. Every wrapping/painting/sheet-metal question is really asking: what is the area of this flat net?

steps.title: Unfold the box
steps.teach: Watch the box open into its net: 6 rectangles, in 3 matching pairs. Add them and you have the total surface — the formula $2(lw + lh + wh)$ is just this picture with the pairs collected.

steps.title: Walls in one multiplication: P × h
steps.body: Going around the prism once covers every wall; unrolled, that walk is a straight line of length $P$ (the base perimeter). The walls form a $P \times h$ rectangle: $S_{lat} = Ph$. Then total $= Ph + 2B$. For the triangle-base prism with legs $6, 8$: hypotenuse $10$ (the 6-8-10 triple), $P = 24$, and with $h = 10$, walls $= 240$, bases $2 \times 24$, total $288$.

steps.title: Paint the pillar
steps.prompt: A square pillar ($a = 2$, height $9$) needs its WALLS painted (not top or bottom). Area to paint?
steps.explanation: $S_{lat} = Ph = (4 \cdot 2) \cdot 9 = 72$. Adding both square ends ($+8$) would give 80 — but nobody paints the underside of a pillar.

examples.prompt: Right prism: base right triangle legs $6, 8$; height $10$. Total surface?

steps.title: Net worth
steps.intro: P × h for walls; don't forget how many bases the problem really wants.

problems.explanation: Walls: $P h = 2(50+30) \cdot 40 = 6400$; bottom: $50 \cdot 30 = 1500$. Total $7900$ — one base only, it's open on top.

problems.prompt: A right prism's walls unroll into a $24 \times 7$ rectangle. Its base perimeter and height are…
problems.explanation: The unrolled width IS the perimeter (the walk around the base); the rectangle's height is the prism's. Convention pins it: $P = 24$, $h = 7$.

steps.title: Surface area of prisms
bullets: Unfold: walls = one P × h rectangle ⟹ S_lat = Ph (right prisms).
Total: S = Ph + 2B; box: S = 2(lw + lh + wh); cube: 6a².
Read the container: sealed = 2 bases, open-top = 1, pipe = 0.
When stuck, DRAW the net — areas can't hide on a flat sheet.

lessons.title: Volume of Prisms
lessons.concreteComparison: A ream of paper is 500 identical sheets: its volume is (area of one sheet) × (height of the stack). Slide the stack into a lean — volume unchanged, it's still the same 500 sheets. That's prism volume, and even the leaning-stack trick has a name: Cavalieri's principle.
lessons.objective: Compute prism volumes with V = B·h, including composite bases, and use Cavalieri's idea for oblique prisms.
concept: A prism is a stack of identical polygon slices. Volume = (slice area) × (stack height): $V = B \cdot h$. For a box: $V = lwh$; for a cube: $V = a^3$.

**Cavalieri's principle**: two solids with equal cross-section areas at every height have equal volumes. Push a stack of paper sideways into a lean — every sheet keeps its area, so the volume stays $B \cdot h$. That's why oblique prisms use the same formula, with $h$ = the TRUE height (perpendicular distance), not the slanted edge.
Composite bases: split the base polygon into rectangles/triangles, add the areas, then multiply by height once. Volume inherits all the area technology from plane geometry.
lessons.keyIdea: V = B·h — base area times true height, for right AND oblique prisms (Cavalieri: leaning doesn't change slice areas).

facts.title: Box and cube
facts.explanation: The base area is just lw (or a²).

facts.title: Cavalieri
facts.explanation: A leaning ream of paper holds the same paper.

workedExamples.statement: A right prism of height $10$ has a right-triangle base with legs $6$ and $8$. Find its volume.
An oblique prism has base area $30$ and lateral edge $12$ leaning at $30°$ to the base plane. Find its volume.
workedExamples.solution: True height $h = 12\sin 30° = 6$ (NOT 12). $V = B h = 30 \cdot 6 = 180$. Cavalieri licenses $B \cdot h$; the lean only means $h$ must be computed, not read off the edge.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying by the lateral edge instead of the true height for oblique prisms.
commonMistakes.correction: V = B·h wants the perpendicular height. Oblique edge $\ell$ at angle $\theta$: $h = \ell\sin\theta$. Using $\ell$ overstates the volume by the factor $1/\sin\theta$.

commonMistakes.text: Doubling the base area 'because there are two bases' (importing surface-area thinking).
commonMistakes.correction: Volume uses the base ONCE — it's slice × height. The '2B' belongs to surface area only. Keep the formulas in separate pockets: S = Ph + 2B, V = Bh.

tryIt.statement: A cube has volume $216$. Find its edge, total surface area, and space diagonal.
tryIt.solution: $a = \sqrt[3]{216} = 6$. Surface: $6a^2 = 216$ (a cube where V and S agree numerically!). Space diagonal: $6\sqrt{3} \approx 10.4$.

tryIt.statement: An L-shaped room: a $6 \times 4$ rectangle with a $2 \times 2$ square bite removed, ceiling height $3$. Find the air volume.
tryIt.solution: Base: $24 - 4 = 20$; $V = 20 \cdot 3 = 60$ cubic meters. Composite base, single height multiplication.

steps.title: Volume = slice × height
steps.body: Every prism is a deck of identical slices. Slice area $B$, deck height $h$: volume $V = Bh$. For boxes this collapses to $lwh$ — the base slice is a $l \times w$ rectangle. No new idea beyond 'how much is one slice, how tall is the pile.'

steps.title: Grow a box
steps.teach: Step $l$, $w$, $h$ and watch $V = lwh$ and $S = 2(lw+lh+wh)$ update live. Notice: doubling ONE dimension doubles the volume, but does NOT double the surface — the two formulas scale differently (that asymmetry becomes a whole lesson in Unit 6).

steps.title: The leaning stack holds the same paper
steps.body: Shove the top of a paper ream sideways: an oblique prism. Sheet count unchanged, sheet areas unchanged ⟹ volume unchanged: still $Bh$ with $h$ the VERTICAL height of the pile. This is Cavalieri's principle — 'equal slices at every level, equal volumes' — and it will quietly power the pyramid and sphere formulas later in the course.

steps.title: The honest height
steps.prompt: An oblique prism: base area $30$, lateral edge $12$ at $30°$ to the base. Volume?
steps.explanation: True height $= 12\sin30° = 6$, so $V = 30 \cdot 6 = 180$. Using the edge itself gives the inflated $360$ — the classic oblique trap.

steps.title: Straight and leaning
steps.intro: B first, then the honest h.

examples.prompt: Right prism: base legs $6, 8$ (right triangle), $h = 10$. Volume?

steps.title: Fill 'er up
steps.intro: Slice area × honest height, every time.

problems.prompt: Prism: equilateral-triangle base of side $4$, height $9$. Volume?
Two prisms share the same base; one stands straight with height $5$, the other leans with lateral edge $5$. Which holds more?
problems.explanation: The leaner's TRUE height is $5\sin\theta < 5$. Equal edges, unequal heights — Cavalieri compares slices at equal HEIGHTS, and the leaning prism simply isn't as tall.

steps.title: Cavalieri, the volume whisperer
steps.body: Bonaventura Cavalieri published his 'method of indivisibles' in 1635 — thirty years before calculus. His slicing principle computes volumes that stumped the Greeks, and modern proofs of the sphere volume formula still route through it: compare the sphere's slices to a cylinder-minus-cone's slices, find them equal at every height, done. You'll do exactly this in Unit 5.

steps.title: Prism volume
bullets: V = B·h: slice area × perpendicular height — right or oblique.
Box: lwh; cube: a³; composite bases: add areas first, multiply once.
Oblique edge ℓ at angle θ: h = ℓ sin θ. Never multiply by the slant.
Cavalieri: equal slices at every level ⟹ equal volumes (the leaning ream).

practice.statement: A prism has $30$ edges. Find its vertex and face counts, and verify Euler's formula.
Find the space diagonal of a cube with edge $8$, and the edge of a cube whose space diagonal is $12$.
A box measures $9 \times 12 \times 8$. Find its space diagonal and total surface area.
A right prism of height $12$ stands on an equilateral triangle of side $6$. Find its lateral surface, total surface, and volume.
A cube's total surface area is $96$. Find its volume and space diagonal.
An oblique prism has a $5 \times 4$ rectangular base and lateral edge $8$ making $60°$ with the base plane. Find its volume.

The three face diagonals of a box from one corner are $\sqrt{34}$, $\sqrt{41}$, and $5$ (from faces $l\times w$, $l \times h$, $w \times h$ with $l=5, w=3, h=4$ — verify), and find the space diagonal.
A swimming pool is a right prism $25$ m long and $10$ m wide, with depth $2$ m. Find the water volume when full and the area of the walls + floor that get tiled.
practice.solution: Volume: $25 \cdot 10 \cdot 2 = 500$ m³. Tiling: floor $250$ + long walls $2(25 \cdot 2) = 100$ + short walls $2(10 \cdot 2) = 40$: total $390$ m² (no ceiling on a pool!).

testYourself.statement: A prism's base is a polygon with $7$ sides. Count vertices, edges, faces; Euler check.
Find the space diagonal of a $2 \times 6 \times 9$ box.
A cube's face diagonal is $6$. Find its space diagonal.
Total surface area of a right prism with height $7$ on a $3 \times 4$ rectangular base?
testYourself.solution: $P = 14$, walls $= 98$; bases $2 \cdot 12 = 24$; total $= 122$. (Same as the box formula: $2(12 + 21 + 28) = 122$ ✓.)

testYourself.statement: An oblique prism: base area $18$, lateral edge $10$ at $45°$ to the base. Volume?
A cube of edge $a$ has volume equal to its total surface area (numerically). Find $a$.
